Foreword 


It  was  my  pleasure  to  provide  a  foreword  for  this  bibliography,  in  which 
Professors  Joseph  L.  Laurenti  and  Alberto  Porqueras-Mayo  have  described  in  such 
detail  the  holdings  in  the  literature  of  the  Spanish  Golden  Age  found  in  the 
University  of  Illinois  Library  at  Urbana-Champaign. 

They  are  to  be  commended  for  providing  in  their  descriptions  not  only  pagina- 
tion and  height  in  centimeters,  but  also  collation  by  signature,  and  reference  to 
published  bibliographies  and  locations  in  other  North  American  libraries. 

In  general  the  collections  of  the  University  of  Illinois  Library's  Rare  Book 
Room  are  strong  in  holdings  of  sixteenth-  and  seventeenth-century  history  and 
literature,  especially  of  England.   The  Spanish  holdings  for  the  same  period  can 
be  considered  complementary  to  the  British  holdings,  and  together  they  are  cultural 
manifestations  of  the  two  main  political  rivals  of  that  time. 

The  earliest  books  represented  in  this  catalog  are  medieval  works  in  Latin, 
by  Spanish  authors  or  printed  in  Spain.   Although  the  main  arrangement  is  not 
chronological,  the  next  period  represented  is  that  of  the  rise  of  vernacular 
literature  in  Western  Europe  during  the  fifteenth  and  sixteenth  centuries — whether 
translations  of  Greek  and  Latin  (and  medieval)  classics  into  Spanish,  or  original 
works  by  such  writers  as  Boscan  (died  1542) ,  Antonio  de  Guevara  (died  about  1545) , 
or  Luis  de  Leon  (died  1591).   These  writers*  works  show  the  development  of  prose 
and  poetical  styles  in  Spanish.  Moreover,  their  works,  and  particularly  those  of 
Antonio  de  Guevara,  were  regarded  highly  enough  outside  of  Spain  to  be  translated 
into  other  vernacular  literatures,  including  English.  A  number  of  Spanish  emblem 
books,  written  at  a  somewhat  later  date  by  Saavedra  Fajardo,  Horozco  y  Covarrubias, 
and  Borja,  have  found  their  way  into  the  Rare  Book  Room  of  the  University  of 
Illinois  Library  over  the  last  several  decades.   The  faculty  of  the  Department  of 


xi 


Foreword 


Spanish,  Italian,  and  Portuguese  was  most  enthusiastic  about  acquiring  books  of 
this  genre  in  Spanish  to  enhance  the  already  strong  emblem  book  collections.  At 
the  same  time  rare  editions  of  picaresque  novels  by  Cervantes,  Aleman,  and  Quevedo 
were  also  acquired.   I  should  mention  that  as  far  as  bibliographic  control  of 
North  American  copies  of  Spanish  literature  of  the  Golden  Age  is  concerned,  this 
catalog  contains  a  number  of  works  not  indicated  by  the  National  Union  Catalog  as 
being  at  the  University  of  Illinois  at  Urb ana -Champaign.   In  this  Library  they  are 
only  partially  cataloged  and  hence  not  recorded,  but  are  as  accessible  to  the  public 
as  any  of  the  cataloged  items  are. 

This  exception  also  applies  to  numerous  works  by  such  authors  as  Ramon  Lull, 
Juan  Luis  Vives,  Pero  Mex£a,  Luis  de  Gongora,  and  Baltasar  Gracian  as  well  as  to 
many  classics  translated  into  Spanish. 

This  catalog  lists  in  excess  of  one  thousand  items  from  the  glorious  Spanish 
Golden  Age  -  works  in  classical  languages  printed  in  Spain,  works  in  the  Spanish 
language  printed  outside  Spain,  and  works  written  by  Spanish  authors.   This  is 
indeed  a  rich  collection. 

N.  Frederick  Nash 

Rare  Book  Room  Librarian 

University  of  Illinois  Library 

Urbana-Champaign 

May  1978 


xii 


Introduction 


After  several  years  of  patient  work  we  have  succeeded  in  compiling  a  catalog 
of  Spanish  rare  books  in  the  library  of  the  University  of  Illinois,  Urbana.   These 
books  date  from  1472  (the  time  of  Saint  Isidorus,  Etymologiae,  Augsburg,  Gilnther 
Zainer)  to  1700.   The  library  of  the  University  of  Illinois  contains  many  rare 
editions,  some  unique,  and  even  some  previously  unknown. 

Until  now,  the  researcher  in  North  America  has  only  had  access  to  two 
specialized  catalogs  covering  Spanish  Golden  Age  literature.   One  was  a  catalog 
which,  beside  including  the  Golden  Age,  encompassed  several  other  centuries: 
Catalogue  of  the  Spanish  Library,  and  of  the  Portuguese  Books  Bequeathed  by  George 
Ticknor  to  the  Boston  Public  Library,  Boston,  1879  (reprinted  by  G.K.  Hall  &  Co., 
Boston,  1972,  an  edition  which  proves  the  perennial  value  of  this  type  of  biblio- 
graphical inventory).   The  other  was  Clara  Louise  Penney,  Printed  Books  1468  -  1700 
in  the  Hispanic  Society  of  America,  New  York,  1965.   Of  course,  there  are  certain 
catalogs  which  cover  specific  literary  genres  of  the  Golden  Age,  for  example: 
Jose  M.  Regueiro,  Spanish  Drama  of  the  Golden  Age,  A  Catalogue  of  the  Comedia 
Collection  in  the  University  of  Pennsylvania  Library,  New  Haven,  1971.   For  this 
present  catalog  we  have  used  as  a  model,  in  many  aspects,  the  format  of  Clara 
Louise  Penney 's  catalog,  especially  with  respect  to  the  following  points: 

1.  All  entries  are  listed  alphabetically  by  author. 

2.  The  same  system  with  respect  to  certain  collective  titles  is  used: 
e.g.,  Aragon,  Bible,  Church,  Spain  (Laws,  statutes,  etc.). 

3.  A  similar  system  is  also  employed  with  respect  to  cross  references. 

4.  The  original  orthography  has  been  retained. 

5.  Other  bibliographical  inventories  which  mention  the  same  edition  are 
indicated:  i.£.  Gallardo,  Palau,  Salva,  Simon  Diaz.  .  . 

These  references,  although  numerous,  are  not  meant  to  be  complete  and  are 
included  simply  as  an  aid  to  the  reader. 

xiii 


Introduction 


Our  catalog,  however,  offers  other  features,  motivated  by  a  desire  to  present 
a  more  inclusive  accumulation  of  data  about  rare  books.   The  following  are  the 
major  differences  which,  we  think,  constitute  definite  advantages  over  Penney 's 
system: 

1.  In  general,  the  complete  title  of  each  book  is  transcribed,  including 
the  whole  title  page.  When  the  copy  housed  at  the  University  of  Illinois 
(lU)  was  without  a  title  page  the  data  was  supplied  from  other  accredited 
bibliographical  sources  such  as  Perez  Pastor,  Medina,  Salva,  etc. 

2.  The  number  of  pages  or  folios  is  indicated. 

3.  In  general  the  signature  collations  are  indicated.   In  this  case  we  have 
been  guided  by  the  books  of  H.M.  Adams  (Catalogue  of  Books  Printed  on  the 
Continent  of  Europe  1501-1600  in  Cambridge  Libraries .   Compiled  by ... , 
Cambridge,  1967,  2  vols.),  and  J.  Peeters-Fontainas  (Bibliographie  des 
impressions  espagnoles  des  Pays-Has  meridionaux.  Wieuw-Koop,  1965,  2  vols.), 
among  others. 

4.  The  ex-libris  which  are  in  the  copies  at  the  University  of  Illinois  are 
indicated  when  they  appear,  as  are  also  colophons  when  present. 

5.  We  have  recorded  as  faithfully  as  possible,  up  to  the  letter  S^,  all  other 
copies  in  the  libraries  of  North  America  recorded  in  the  Union  Catalog, 
which,  as  of  this  introduction,  is  still  in  the  process  of  publication. 
As  the  subsequent  volumes  of  the  Union  Catalog  appear,  the  other  North 
American  holdings  will  be  incorporated  either  as  an  appendix  or,  if 
possible,  as  a  supplement  to  a  future  edition.  Where  the  Union  Catalog 
terminates,  other  catalogs,  have  been  used  to  locate  additional  copies 

in  other  public  and  university  libraries. 

6.  In  order  to  indicate  the  different  North  American  libraries  we  have  used 
the  initial  letter  abbreviations  of  the  Union  Catalog. 

7 .  We  have  added  an  Index  of  Printers ,  indicating  in  chronological  order  the 
abbreviated  titles  of  printed  books. 

We  hope  to  offer  with  this  work  a  reference  manual  for  librarians  and  those 
interested  in  the  humanities,  since  the  books  which  are  included  in  this  catalog 
are  not  only  literary  but  also  concern  religion,  law,  politics,  etc.,  including 
the  sciences  (medicine,  astronomy,  etc.).   Besides  those  involved  in  Spanish 
literature,  those  having  contact  with  other  literatures,  especially  English, 
Italian,  and  French,  will  be  able,  through  the  translations  listed  here,  to  be- 
come more  involved  in  the  field  of  comparative  studies.   In  order  to  make  the 
catalog  available  most  expeditiously  and  efficiently,  we  have  supplied  typed 
camera-ready  copy  to  the  publisher. 


xiv 


Introduction 


Although,  as  we  have  indicated,  additional  copies  in  North  American  libraries 
are  listed,  it  should  be  noted  that  our  mention  of  other  inventories  (e.g.,  Adams 
Peeters-Fontainas,  Simon  Diaz,  and  others)  will  lead  the  reader  to  holdings  in 
other  libraries  dispersed  around  the  world. 

We  recommend  that  the  specialist  consult  our  various  published  works, 
mentioned  in  the  List  of  References  below,  because  there  the  chronological 
scheme  has  been  enlarged,  at  times,  to  include  the  nineteenth  century,  and  other 
holdings  in  European  libraries  are  noted. •'■ 

Our  list  of  thanks  would  be  very  extensive  were  we  to  mention  everyone  who 
helped  us,  but  we  wish  especially  to  make  known  our  gratitude  to  the  Research  Board 
of  the  University  of  Illinois  and  to  our  research  assistant.  Miss  Sherilyn  Freeman. 
Also  we  wish  to  express  our  appreciation  for  the  assistance  given  to  us  for  many 
years  by  the  staff  of  the  University  of  Illinois  Library,  above  all  by  the  Head 
Librarian,  Mr.  N.  Frederick  Nash,  and  Mrs.  Mary  S.  Ceibert,  the  librarian  of  the 
Rare  Books  Library. 

We  hope,  thus,  that  this  catalog  will  be  an  essential  resource  for  all  those 
researchers  directly  or  indirectly  interested  in  these  nearly  two  hundred  glorious 
years  of  Western  culture  and  civilization. 

A.  P-M.

Titles,  v/ith  Cardinal  Jimenez'  coat  of  arms,  are  A^ithin  woodcut 
borders. 

Initials;  tail-pieces. 

Vol.  I.  has  Hebrew,  Greek  and  Latin  in  parallel  columns,  with 
Ghaldaic  text  at  foot  of  page;  vols.  2-4  are  viithout  Ghaldaic; 
vol.  6  has  Greek  and  Latin  only. 

Vols.  1  and  2-4  have  colophon  (vol.  4)  dated,  10  July  1517; 
vol.  6  has  two  colophons  dated,  respectively,  17  March  1515  and 
51  May  1515. 

The  New  Testamente  (vol.  5)  has  colophon  dated,  10  January  1514. 

The  first  polyglot  Bible,  edited  by  eminent  scholars,  headed  by 
Diego  Lopez  de  Zuniga,  and  printed,  in  an  edition  of  600  copies,  at 
the  expense  of  Cardinal  Jimenez.

Text  lacks  Caput  XXIX.  Gf.  Migne .  Patrologia.  Ser.  Latina,  vol. 
98,  pp.  990-12^8. 

The  first  part  knovm  as  the  Libri  Carolini.  is  a  manifesto 
condemning  the  second  Council  of  Nicaea  in  785* 

The  second  part  is  an  attack  against  the  heresy  of  the 
archbishop  of  Toledo,  Elipandus,  and  the  Bishop  Felix  of  Urdel,who 
claimed  that  Jesus  Christ  in  only  the  adoptive  son  of  God. 

Part  I  and  part  II  edited  by  Jean  du  Tillet,  from  a  manuscript 
of  the  Bibliotheque  de  I'Arsenalof  Paris. 

Extremely  rare.  See  British  Mu'^eum  Catalogue  of  French  Book,  p.  105 
and  British  Museum  Catalogue  of  German  Books,  p.  674,

'i^RACIAN  DANTISCO,  Lucas,  fl.  1590.  /  Galateo  espanol,   £ng.  tr.  by 
William  3tyle_7.   Galateo  Espagnol.  Or,  The  Spanish  Gallant, 
Inscrvctinp:  Thee  in  that  which  thou  must  doe,  and  take  heed 
of  in  thy  usuall  cariagie,  to  be  well  esteeaed,and  loved  of  the 
//)\  j^?.   People.  Written  in  Spanish  by  Lucas  Gracian  de  Antisco  servant 
,  0        to  his  Ma.jesty.  And  done  into  English  b;i  W.  3.  of  the  Inner  Temple 
Esquire.  Full  of  variety,  and  delight,  and  very  necessary  to  be 
perused,  not  only  of  the  generous  youth  of  this  Kingdom,  but 
also  of  all  such  as  are  exercised  in  their  gentile  Education. 
London,  Printed  by  E,  G.  for  William  Lee  at  the  Turkes  head 
in  Fleete-streete,  neere  to  the  Mirer  Taverne.  1640.  11  p.  1. 
209  (i«£»  225),  4  1.  With  copper  plate  frontispiece  showing 
a  gallant  in  full  regalia.  15cm. 

The  very  rare  first  English  edition  of  the  popular  Spanish 
book  of  the  courtier,  translated  by  William  Style,  the  noted 
17th  century  English  legalist. 

Slightly  worn,  but  generally  in  excellent  condition. 

Colophon:  "Imprimatur  Tho.  Wykes."

North's  translation,  first  published  in  1557  (see  infra  ), 
although  professedly  from  the  French,  was  made  in  large  measure 
from  the  Spanish  original.  Cf.  A.F.  Allison's  English  Translations 
...,  1974,p.  89.  The  fourth  book  is  a  translation  of  Guevara's 
Aviso  de  Privados  2.   Doctrina  de  Cortesanos.  Here  North  used  the 
French  version  of  Jacques  de  Rochemore,  Lyons,  1556. 

The  fourth  book  has  special  title  -  page  not  included  in  the 
paging.

Manuscript  note  on  fly  leaf:  "This  pastoral  romance  is  said 
to  be  the  foundation  of  Shakespeare's  Two  Gentlemen  of  Verona 
and  of  Pyraaus  and  Thisbe  in  Midsummer  Night ' s  Dream.  It  is 
also  said  to  have  been  partly  translated  by  Sir  Philip  Sidney." 

Part  2  is  a  translation  of  the  Diana  of  Alonzo  Perez,  and 
part  5  is  a  translation  of  the  Diana  enamorada  of  Gaspar  Gil 
Polo.

An  illustrated  Bible  found  complete.  Of  the  ten  copies  in  the 
Stillwell  Census,  six  are  single  volumes  or  fragments  of  volumes 
only.  The  British  Museum  set  is  made  up  of  four  odd  volumes, 
different  in  size  from  ours.  Five  of  the  six  copies  reported  by 
Polain  are  incomplete,  as  are  four  of  the  seven  in  Pellechet.  All 
of  the  woodcuts  are   present  in  our  copy.

and  Juan  Bianco,  c^.  ;1482  //   196  1.    50cm. 


Signatures:  a-x  , 

A  variant  of  Hain, 15704,  without  place  and  date.  Bound  in 
contemporary  brown  half  morocco  and  oak  boards,  traces  of  clasps. 

The  text  of  this  rare  edition  is  identical  with  the  edition  dated 
Nov.  12,  1482  (Zaragoza,  Paul  Hurus  and  Juan  Blanco),  and  contains 
the  same  typographical  characteristics.   Pol.  aij  is  adorned  with 
an  ornamental  border  made  up  of  flowers,  birds,  and  a  snail.   This 
border  is  perhaps  the  first  to  have  appeared  in  Spanish  incunabulae. 

474 


The  punctuation  marks  are  not  to  be  fmmd  in  any  other  L- 

production  during  this  period.   Finally  the  words,  "(Be)atus 

vir  qui  no  abit"  on  aiii.i   recto,  "I are  fremuerunt"  on  aiii.i 

verso,  "(Do)niine  cur  multiplicati  sunt  qui"  on  ^v  recto,  and 

"Iffl  inuocarem"  on  av  verso  are  absolutely  different  from  the 

types  of  the  text,  and  appear  to  be  engraved  on  wood.  This 

is  not  true  of  the  capitals,  which  are  the  same  as  those  in  the 

text. 

Goff,  T-529;  Haebler,  no.  651;  Stillwell,  T-477
